ut:template.p: This test case is to be carried out on a .
Follow the installation steps at IoT installation media
If all actions produce the expected results listed, please submit a 'passed' result.
If any action fails, or produces an unexpected result, please submit a 'failed' result and file a bug. Please be sure to include the bug number when you submit your result.

ut:test.dd: Check that the Ubuntu logo, and spinner appear during boot time

ut:test.dt: Ensure you have speakers on your monitor or headphones plugged into it
ut:test.dd: Check that the Ubuntu start up sound plays through the monitor's audio output before the initial System Configuration appears

ut:test.dt: Select your timezone, and click on the Continue button
Input your initial user details and password admin can not be used - it is a dedicated Linux User
ut:test.dd: Name, username and password are accepted. Login options and home folder encryption choices shown
Exit code is clean (0) and no error messages are reported

ut:test.dt: Click the power icon at the top right of the screen, and expand the "Power Off / Log Out" entry in the menu that appears, then "Restart..." from that menu, and confirm "Restart" in the dialog that appears
ut:test.dd: System reboots successfully to a login prompt

ut:test.dt: Click the power icon at the top right of the screen, and expand the "Power Off / Log Out" entry in the menu that appears, then "Power Off..." from that menu, and confirm "Power Off" in the dialog that appears
ut:test.dd: System shuts down in a reasonable time (less than a minute)

ut:test.dt:
Perform a large (300-600MB) file copy to USB storage
Generate a large (500MB) file: dd if=/dev/urandom of=rubbish bs=1M count=500
Insert a USB stick (appropriately sized) into a spare USB port
Make a mount directory: sudo mkdir /mnt/stick
Mount the stick: sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/stick (modify mount-point as necessary; check sudo dmesg output if unsure)
Copy the file: sudo cp rubbish /mnt/stick/
Unmount the stick: sudo umount /mnt/stick
Remove the stick from the USB port
Re-insert the stick into the USB port
Re-mount the stick: sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/stick (again, adjust mount-point as necessary)
Compare the copied file to that on the stick: cmp rubbish /mnt/stick/rubbish
ut:test.dd: cmp returns 0 and outputs nothing indicating the files are identical

ut:test.dt: Click the power icon at the top right of the screen, select Settings from the menu that appears, then "About" in the left panel of the window that appears
ut:test.dd: Reported "Memory" is consistent with a . It should be in the region of .

ut:test.dt: Boot with a monitor attached to both HDMI ports
ut:test.dd: Verify that you see output on both monitors

ut:test.dt:
Check auto-configuration of ethernet
Run ip addr
Check that a valid IP address is recorded on the interface
Check ping google.com successfully pings a few times (Ctrl+C to cancel)
ut:test.dd: The "" interface should have a DHCP assigned IP address and you should be able to ping google.com

ut:test.dt:
Configure wifi via Network Manager
Click the power icon at the top right of the screen
Select the WiFi entry from the menu and Select Network under that
Select your local WiFi network from the scan list
Enter the password for your local WiFi network when prompted
Wait a few seconds (to allow DHCP to complete), then run ip addr
Check that a valid IP address is recorded on the interface
Check ping google.com successfully pings a few times (Ctrl+C to cancel)
ut:test.dd: The "" interface should have a DHCP assigned IP address and you should be able to ping google.com

ut:test.dt:
Start Firefox and play a YouTube video
Ensure you have functioning speakers / a headset plugged into your monitor
Click on the Firefox icon on the left of the screen
Navigate to YouTube
Select a video (with audio!) to play
ut:test.dd: Check the video plays smoothly, and that audio is output through the monitor, or speakers / headset plugged into the monitor

ut:test.dt:
Download and play BigBuckBunny in the built-in video player
Ensure you have functioning speakers / a headset plugged into your monitor
Start a terminal session
Run wget https://archive.org/download/BigBuckBunny_124/Content/big_buck_bunny_720p_surround.mp4
Once the download has completed, run totem big_buck_bunny_720p_surround.mp4
ut:test.dd: Check the video plays smoothly, and that audio is output through the monitor, or speakers / headset plugged into the monitor

ut:test.dt: Press Super+L and wait for the lock screen to appear, then fade, then for the monitor to suspend. Move the mouse to wake up the monitor, then enter your password to unlock the desktop.
ut:test.dd: Ensure the monitor suspends correctly, that it awakens again correctly, and that the desktop unlocks successfully (without the system hanging).

ut:define.name: post-install
ut:define.p: Before booting your CM4 with the new image, edit config.txt on the boot (1st) partition and uncomment the #dtoverlay=dwc2,dr_mode=host line to ensure the USB ports on the IO board operate correctly

ut:define.name: post-install
ut:define.p: Before booting your CM4 with the new image, edit config.txt on the boot (1st) partition and uncomment the #dtoverlay=dwc2,dr_mode=host line to ensure the USB ports on the IO board operate correctly

ut:define.name: post-install
ut:define.p: Before booting your CM4 with the new image, edit config.txt on the boot (1st) partition and uncomment the #dtoverlay=dwc2,dr_mode=host line to ensure the USB ports on the IO board operate correctly
